testcase: Cant we release an sdk for an old api-version?
query:
title: Cant we release an sdk for an old api-version?

question: Hi Azure SDK Onboarding,

My team, AzureStackHCI-VM is working on releasing our sdks for the first time. I recently worked on creating beta sdks for our latest preview api-version (2025-06-01-preview)

Our GA/stable api-version is quite old (2024-01-01) but we want to create an sdk for that as well.
But in this comment, I am told that we are not allowed to release an old sdk version?
https://github.com/Azure/sdk-release-request/issues/6525

What is your recommendation?
ground_truth:
SDK versioning follows a single track and typically allows only forward releases. However, since your earlier versions are all previews, this is not a blocker in your situation.  
The major problem is switching between TypeSpec and Swagger,  which will introduce unexpected breaking changes.
If possible, please consider converting the 2024-01-01 version to TypeSpec and proceed with SDK releases based on that.
If you’re planning to release a new GA version soon, it may be better to skip the stable version and wait for the next GA release. Regarding the s360 flagging, if you’re simply pursuing signoff, I suggest skipping the stable version in Swagger and waiting for your next GA version in TypeSpec.

query:
title: Azure SDK for Microsoft.Applink

question: Hi Team, We are in Private Preview phase for our new RP, Microsoft.Applink and as part of that we have our RP specs in `azure-rest-api-specs-pr` repo - [azure-rest-api-specs-pr/specification/applink/AppLink.Management at RPSaaSMaster · Azure/azure-rest…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/tree/RPSaaSMaster/specification/applink/AppLink.Management)
 
We want to be able to work on CLI for our initial set of customers and wondering how to onboard onto azure sdk for the same?

can you please help with the process of generating sdk for RPs in private preview phase? we would want to build up CLI on top of the SDKs.
ground_truth:
we don't support the release of SDKs during private preview
'Is there a separate process that has to be followed for CLI, PowerShell, and Terraform updates or is it part of the SDK release plan?'
No, CLI updates are not part of the SDK release. For information relevant to CLI, PowerShell, or Terraform please review instructions here: [aka.ms/azureclitools/](https://aka.ms/azureclitools/onboarding)

query:
title: New Product for New GA?

question: We did our last GA in 2024-04-01 and we added a product in serviceTree to match this version.

We are now planning for our next GA, do we need a new product to be added in ServiceTree? Of we can reuse the same product?

[Service Tree | Products | Billing RP - 2024-04-01](https://microsoftservicetree.com/products/7269b016-8af0-4539-9183-91dfbf1df197)
ground_truth: The practice of creating an offering record for an API version is incorrect.  The team may need to understand better of service tree entities.  Refer to this page [ServiceTree Products | Service Lifecycle & Actions Team](https://eng.ms/docs/cloud-ai-platform/azure-core/azure-core-product/product-lifecycle-management-plm/service-lifecycle-and-actions-team/service-lifecycle-actions-team/cloudringsstrategy/stproducttaxonomy).  The user question is not in SDK onboarding domain, it is a question of CPEX onboarding, refer to [Announcement | Service Lifecycle & Actions Team](https://eng.ms/docs/cloud-ai-platform/azure-core/azure-core-product/product-lifecycle-management-plm/service-lifecycle-and-actions-team/service-lifecycle-actions-team/CPEX/home).  User should seek the answer from CPEX support https://aka.ms/cpexsupport

expected_references.title: ServiceTree Products | Service Lifecycle & Actions Team

testcase: Question on processes about developing a new RP
query:
title: Question on processes about developing a new RP

question: Hi team,
 
We are introducing a new resource provider and have the API specs already reviewed by ARM in a private branch on https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr
 
PR - [adding Microsoft.ComputeLimit typespec by parthshah2305 · Pull Request #24115 · Azure/azure-rest-ap…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/24115)
 
I have a few naive questions around the next steps and process. 
We are still working on the related RP onboarding and manifest related steps. In addition, the service side changes are still in progress. 
 
When is the right time to merge API specs in the main branch of the [Azure/azure-rest-api-specs: The source for REST API specifications for Microsoft Azure.](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs) ?
 
Does SDK generation requires the typespec changes to be in the main branch?
ground_truth:
[Azure REST API specs repositories on GitHub](https://eng.ms/docs/products/azure-developer-experience/design/api-specs-pr/api-repos)

Also, have you onboarded to the Azure SDK Release Planner yet? If not, that should be step 1 and then create a release plan. It will walk you through the process step by step and is a mandatory requirement to exit Cloud Lifecycle phases.

expected_references.title: Azure REST API specs repositories on GitHub

query:
title: Folder layout in source repos

question: Onboarding feels like the right place for this question...
 
I'm looking to understand where the "right" place in the source repos to put a new SDK is (Technically, was...).
 
When I added the new VoiceLive SDK's I'd put them in \sdk\ai\Azure.AI.VoiceLive (C#) with the other Azure.AI SDK's. One reasoning behind this is push in our division to be more uniform in what we present to customers and while "one sdk" in packaging is not the goal, shipping our org chart with a plethora of edges also isn't.
 
But the tooling in the various repos seems very opinionated that being in the same L1 folder means everything is moderately coupled. And looking in some docs the L1 definition was "service"  but I'm not sure how to read that in a world where the push in our division is for all services to share one Azure Portal resource and live behind one APIM gateway that routes to a decent number of back end clusters from a single https://resource.dnsname.com "endpoint". So is that service from the dev team's view, the view we want the customer to have, or some blending of both?
 
Could also be we've used the ci / test yamls wrong and we can have less coupled L2 directories and release pipelines.
 
Anyways, looking for opinions / thoughts / best practice here... Thanks.
ground_truth:
For the azure-rest-api-specs repo, you should follow this folder structure: https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/wiki/Azure-Service-Versioning-Guideline
 
But this doesn't specify the structure in the SDK repos.

query:
title: SDK Breaking change trigger

question: Hi,
If we merge our swaggers to public main and then find the need to make changes, for example in our client tsp, or corrections in our swagger, what is the process? We are needing to merge main for API docs to become available, but take an extension on SDK generation for our public preview release, and I want to ensure that if we do happen to find some changes to make SDK side what we can expect to trigger various processes and cause delays for us.
Thank you in advance!
ground_truth:
Once you merge your OpenAPI (Swagger) specifications to the public main branch, your API docs will be published and available to customers. If you later need to make corrections or updates—whether in your Swagger spec or in your TypeSpec client configuration—the process is as follows:
1.Spec Changes (Swagger or TypeSpec):
Any changes to your API spec (including bug fixes, corrections, or updates) must be made via a new pull request targeting the main branch of the public repository (Azure/azure-rest-api-specs).
You must ensure all required checks pass, and at least one team member with write access approves the PR. Once merged, the updated spec will be published and available for SDK generation and API docs.
If you need to move changes from a private repo to public, you must manually copy the files and open a new PR in the public repo, referencing the review history from the private repo.
2.SDK Generation Timing:
SDK generation is not automatically triggered when you merge spec changes. You control when to generate SDKs, typically via the Azure SDK Tools Agent or Release Planner.
If you delay SDK generation (e.g., for public preview), subsequent spec changes will only affect SDKs generated after those changes are merged. If you generate SDKs from an older spec, they will not include later corrections.
When you are ready to generate SDKs, ensure your spec is up to date and all desired changes are merged.
3.Impact of Subsequent Changes:
If you make further changes after the initial merge, you must repeat the PR process. Each new spec merge may require you to regenerate SDKs to reflect the latest API definition.
If you generate SDKs before all corrections are merged, you may need to regenerate and re-release SDKs after merging additional changes, which can cause delays.
Breaking changes or versioning issues detected by automation may block your PR and require additional review, further delaying SDK generation and release.
4.Release Process and Delays:
The SDK release process requires that your API spec is stable and merged. Any changes after SDK generation may require repeating validation, testing, and review steps.
For GA releases, additional requirements such as APIView approval and updated changelogs must be met.
Best Practice:
To minimize delays, batch your corrections and ensure your spec is as stable as possible before triggering SDK generation. Use the Azure SDK Tools Agent to validate and generate SDKs locally before opening PRs.
Monitor the impact of breaking changes and versioning issues, as these can block merges and require approval from the Azure Breaking Changes Board.
In summary, merging to main makes your API docs available, but SDK generation is a separate, controlled process. Any subsequent spec changes require new PRs and may necessitate regenerating SDKs, potentially causing delays if not managed carefully.
